steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The study found two things: (1) chimpanzees' vitamin D levels correlate with UVB irradiance, and (2) vitamin D levels show no evidence of plateauing even at the highest local UVB levels. A plateau would mean synthesis has hit its ceiling. No plateau means the chimpanzees could still produce more vitamin D if UVB were higher. The blank must follow logically from this.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — averaged across seasons, vitamin D levels in zoo chimpanzees in mid-latitude countries such as Norway tend to be comparable to those in wild chimpanzees in tropical countries such as Liberia.: The passage establishes that mid-latitude countries have lower and more variable UVB than tropical ones, so comparable levels would contradict the correlation finding. ✗
  • Choice B — adaptations to seasonal variations in UVB irradiance may be emerging in zoo chimpanzees in Norway and other mid-latitude countries.: The passage says wild chimpanzees lack such adaptations. Speculating about emerging adaptations goes beyond what the no-plateau finding supports. ✗
  • Choice C — zoo chimpanzees in Norway and other mid-latitude countries tend to synthesize less vitamin D than they are inherently capable of synthesizing.: Since there is no plateau even at the highest local UVB, the chimpanzees' synthesis capacity has not been reached under Norway's lower UVB. They would be capable of synthesizing more vitamin D under stronger UVB exposure. ✓
  • Choice D — providing supplemental vitamin D to chimpanzees in zoos in Norway and other mid-latitude countries would likely not be beneficial.: The no-plateau finding does not support a claim that supplementation would be unhelpful; it only supports that local UVB exposure has not maxed out synthesis. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
